## IMPROVED DENOISING DIFFUSION PROBABILISTIC MODELS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

We explore denoising diffusion probabilistic models, a class of generative models which have recently been shown to produce excellent samples in the image and audio domains. While these models produce excellent samples, it has yet to be shown that they can achieve competitive log-likelihoods. We show that, with several small modifications, diffusion models can achieve competitive log-likelihoods in the image domain while maintaining high sample quality. Additionally, our models allow for sampling with an order of magnitude fewer diffusion steps with only a modest difference in sample quality. Finally, we explore how sample quality and log-likelihood scale with the number of diffusion steps and the amount of model capacity. We conclude that denoising diffusion probabilistic models are a promising class of generative models with excellent scaling properties and sample quality.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Sohl-Dickstein et al. (2015) introduced diffusion probabilistic models ("diffusion models" for brevity), a class of generative models which match a data distribution by learning to reverse a gradual, multi-step noising process. More recently, Ho et al. (2020) showed an equivalence between these models and score based generative models (Song & Ermon, 2019; 2020), which learn a gradient of the log-density of the data distribution using denoising score matching (Hyvärinen, 2005). It has recently been shown that this class of models can produce high-quality images (Ho et al., 2020; Song & Ermon, 2020; Jolicoeur-Martineau et al., 2020) and audio (Chen et al., 2020b; Kong et al., 2020), but it has yet to be shown that diffusion models can achieve competitive log-likelihoods. Furthermore, while Ho et al. (2020) showed extremely good results on the CIFAR-10 (Krizhevsky, 2009) and LSUN (Yu et al., 2015) datasets, it is unclear how well diffusion models scale to datasets with higher diversity such as ImageNet. Finally, while Chen et al. (2020b) found that diffusion models can efficiently generate audio using a small number of sampling steps, it has yet to be shown that the same is true for images.

In this paper, we show that diffusion models can achieve competitive log-likelihoods while maintaining good sample quality, even on high-diversity datasets like ImageNet. Additionally, we show that our improved models can produce competitive samples an order of magnitude faster than those from Ho et al. (2020). We achieve these results by combining a simple reparameterization of the reverse process variance, a hybrid learning objective that combines the variational lower-bound with the simplified objective from Ho et al. (2020), and a novel noise schedule which allows the model to better leverage the entire diffusion process.

We find surprisingly that, with our hybrid objective, our models obtain better log-likelihoods than those obtained by optimizing the log-likelihood directly, and discover that the latter objective has much more gradient noise during training. We show that a simple importance sampling technique reduces this noise and allows us to achieve better log-likelihoods than with the hybrid objective. Using our trained models, we study how sample quality and log-likelihood change as we adjust the number of diffusion steps used at sampling time. We demonstrate that our improved models allow us to use an order of magnitude fewer steps at test time with only a modest change in sample quality and log-likelihood, thus speeding up sampling for use in practical applications.
Finally, we evaluate the performance of these models as we increase model size, and observe trends that suggest predictable improvements in performance as we increase training compute.

## 2 DENOISING DIFFUSION PROBABILISTIC MODELS

We briefly review the formulation of diffusion models from Ho et al. (2020). This formulation makes various simplifying assumptions, such as a fixed noising process _q_ which adds diagonal Gaussian noise at each timestep. For a more general derivation, see Sohl-Dickstein et al. (2015).

## 3 IMPROVING THE LOG-LIKELIHOOD

While Ho et al. (2020) found that diffusion models can generate high-fidelity samples according to FID (Heusel et al., 2017) and Inception Score (Salimans et al., 2016), they were unable to achieve competitive log-likelihoods with these models. Log-likelihood is a widely used metric in generative modeling, and it is generally believed that optimizing log-likelihood forces generative models to capture all of the modes of the data distribution (Razavi et al., 2019). Additionally, recent work (Henighan et al., 2020) has shown that small improvements in log-likelihood can have a dramatic impact on sample quality and learnt feature representations. Thus, it is important to explore why diffusion models seem to perform poorly on this metric, since this may suggest a fundamental shortcoming such as bad mode coverage. This section explores several modifications to the algorithm described in Section 2 that, when combined, allow diffusion models to achieve much better log-likelihoods on image datasets, suggesting that these models enjoy the same benefits as other likelihood-based generative models.

To study the effects of different modifications, we train fixed model architectures with fixed hyperparameters (Appendix A) on the ImageNet 64 _×_ 64 (van den Oord et al., 2016a) and CIFAR-10 (Krizhevsky, 2009) datasets. While CIFAR-10 has seen more usage for this class of models, we chose to study ImageNet 64 _×_ 64 as well because it provides a good trade-off between diversity and resolution, allowing us to train models quickly without worrying about overfitting. Additionally, ImageNet 64 _×_ 64 has been studied extensively in the context of generative modeling (van den Oord et al., 2016b; Menick & Kalchbrenner, 2018; Child et al., 2019; Roy et al., 2020), allowing us to compare diffusion models directly to many other generative models.

The setup from Ho et al. (2020) (optimizing _L_ simple while setting _σt_[2][=] _[β][t]_[and] _[ T]_[=][1000][) achieves] a log-likelihood of **3.99 bits/dim** on ImageNet 64 _×_ 64 after 200K training iterations. We found in early experiments that we could get a boost in log-likelihood by increasing _T_ from 1000 to 4000; with this change, the log-likelihood improves to **3.77 bits/dim** . For the remainder of this section, we use _T_ = 4000, but we explore this choice in Section 4.

This suggests that, in the limit of infinite diffusion steps, the choice of _σt_ might not matter at all for sample quality. In other words, as we add more diffusion steps, the model mean _µθ_ ( _xt, t_ ) determines the distribution much more than Σ _θ_ ( _xt, t_ ).

While the above argument suggests that fixing _σt_ is a reasonable choice for the sake of sample quality, it says nothing about log-likelihood. In fact, Figure 1b shows that the first few steps of the diffusion process contribute the most to the variational lower bound. Thus, it seems likely that we could improve log-likelihood by using a better choice of Σ _θ_ ( _xt, t_ ). To achieve this, we must learn Σ _θ_ ( _xt, t_ ) without the instabilities encountered by Ho et al. (2020).

Since Figure 1a shows that the reasonable range for Σ _θ_ ( _xt, t_ ) is very small, it would be hard for a neural network to predict Σ _θ_ ( _xt, t_ ) directly, even in the log domain, as observed by Ho et al. (2020). Instead, we found it better to parameterize the variance as an interpolation between _βt_ and _β_[˜] _t_ in the log domain. In particular, our model outputs a vector _v_ containing one component per dimension, and we turn this output into variances as follows:

We did not apply any constraints on _v_ , theoretically allowing the model to predict variances outside of the interpolated range. However, we did not observe the network doing this in practice, suggesting that the bounds for Σ _θ_ ( _xt, t_ ) are indeed expressive enough.

Since _L_ simple doesn’t depend on Σ _θ_ ( _xt, t_ ), we define a new hybrid objective:

For our experiments, we set _λ_ = 0 _._ 001 to prevent _L_ vlb from overwhelming _L_ simple. Along this same line of reasoning, we also apply a stop-gradient to the _µθ_ ( _xt, t_ ) output for the _L_ vlb term. This way, _L_ vlb can guide Σ _θ_ ( _xt, t_ ) while _L_ simple is still the main source of influence over _µθ_ ( _xt, t_ ).

In particular, we selected _s_ such that _[√] β_ 0 was slightly smaller than the pixel bin size, 1 _/_ 127 _._ 5. We chose to use _cos_[2] in particular because it is a common mathematical function with the shape we were looking for. This choice was arbitrary, and we expect that many other functions with similar shapes would work as well.

## 3.3 REDUCING GRADIENT NOISE

We expected to achieve the best log-likelihoods by optimizing _L_ vlb directly, rather than by optimizing _L_ hybrid. However, we were surprised to find that _L_ vlb was actually quite difficult to optimize in practice, at least on the diverse ImageNet 64 _×_ 64 dataset. Figure 4a shows the learning curves for both _L_ vlb and _L_ hybrid. Both curves are noisy, but the hybrid objective clearly achieves better log-likelihoods on the training set given the same amount of training time.

We hypothesized that the gradient of _L_ vlb was much noisier than that of _L_ hybrid. We confirmed this by evaluating the gradient noise scales (McCandlish et al., 2018) for models trained with both objectives, as shown in Figure 4b. Thus, we sought out a way to reduce the variance of _L_ vlb in order to optimize directly for log-likelihood.

Noting that different terms of _L_ vlb have greatly different magnitudes (Figure 1b), we hypothesized that sampling _t_ uniformly causes unnecessary noise in the _L_ vlb objective. To address this, we employ importance sampling:

Since _E_ [ _L_[2] _t_[]][is][unknown][beforehand][and][may][change][throughout][training,][we][maintain][a][history] of the previous 10 values for each loss term, and update this dynamically during training. At the beginning of training, we sample _t_ uniformly until we draw 10 samples for every _t ∈_ [0 _, T −_ 1]. With this importance sampled objective, we are able to achieve our best log-likelihoods by optimizing _L_ vlb.[1] This can be seen in Figure 4a as the " _Lvlb_ (resampled)" curve. The figure also shows that the importance sampled objective is considerably less noisy than the original, uniformly sampled objective.

## 3.4 RESULTS AND ABLATIONS

In this section, we ablate the changes we have made to achieve better log-likelihoods. Table 1 summarizes the results of our ablations on ImageNet 64 _×_ 64, and Table 2 shows them for CIFAR10. We also trained our best ImageNet 64 _×_ 64 models for 1.5M iterations, and report these results as well. Based on the results, we recommend always using the cosine schedule, and the _L_ hybrid objective in most cases. If one is only optimizing for likelihood and not sample quality, the importance sampled _L_ vlb is the best objective to use.

## 4 IMPROVING SAMPLING SPEED

All of our models were trained with 4000 diffusion steps, and thus producing a single sample takes several minutes on a modern GPU. In this section, we explore how performance scales if we reduce the steps used during sampling, and find that our pre-trained models can produce high-quality samples with many fewer diffusion steps than they were trained with without any fine-tuning. Reducing the steps in this way makes it possible to sample from our models in a number of seconds rather than minutes, and greatly improves the practical applicability of image diffusion models.

For a model trained with _T_ diffusion steps, we would typically sample using the same set of _t_ values (1 _,_ 2 _, ..., T_ ) as used during training. However, it is also possible to sample using an arbitrary set of _t_ values. We define a sequence _S_ of _t_ values to use for sampling, such as a strided schedule like ¯ _S_ schedule= (1 _,_ 3 _α_ ¯ _,S_ 5 _t, ..., T_ , which can be used to obtain corresponding sampling variances _−_ 1). Given the training noise schedule _αt_ , we can obtain the sampling noise

We can compute _p_ ( _xSt−_ 1 _|xSt_ ) as _N_ ( _µθ_ ( _xSt, St_ ) _,_ Σ _θ_ ( _xSt, St_ )). Note that Σ _θ_ ( _xSt, St_ ) is parameterized as a range between _βSt_ and _β_[˜] _St_ so it will automatically be rescaled for the shorter diffusion process.

To evaluate sample quality for reduced numbers of sampling steps, we use a stride _K_ over timesteps to reduce the total number of sampling steps from _T_ to _T/K_ . In Figures 5a and 5c, we evaluate FIDs for an _L_ hybrid model and an _L_ simple model that were trained with 4000 diffusion steps, using 30, 50, 100, 150, 200, 400, and 4000 sampling steps. We do this for multiple checkpoints throughout training. We find that the _L_ simple model suffers much more in sample quality when using a reduced number of sampling steps, whereas our _L_ hybrid model maintains sample quality. Furthermore, we find that using more sampling steps becomes increasingly beneficial throughout training. However, 100 sampling steps is still sufficient to achieve near-optimal FIDs for our fully trained models.

In initial experiments, we found that although constant striding did not significantly affect FID, it drastically reduced log-likelihood. To address this, we use a strided subset of timesteps as for FID (with stride _K_ ), but we also include every _t_ from 1 to _T/K_ . This requires _T/K_ extra evaluation steps, but greatly improves log-likelihood compared to the uniformly strided schedule. In Figures 5b and 5d we present log-likelihoods with this modified strided schedule.

To measure how performance scales with compute, we train four different models on ImageNet 64 _×_ 64 with the _L_ hybrid objective described in Section 3.1. To change model capacity, we apply a depth multiplier across all layers, such that the first layer has either 64, 96, 128, or 192 channels. Note that our previous experiments used 128 channels in the first layer. Since the depth of each layer affects the scale of the initial weights, we scale the Adam learning rate for each model by 1 _/[√]_ channel multiplier, such that the 128 channel model has a learning rate of 0.0001 (as in our other experiments).

Figure 6a and 6b show how FID and NLL improve relative to compute. These plots reveal that, to achieve optimal performance for a given amount of compute, it often makes sense to train a larger model for fewer iterations, rather than training a smaller model to convergence. We note that these models do not achieve optimal log-likelihoods because they were trained with our _L_ hybrid objective and not directly with _L_ vlb to keep both good log-likelihoods and sample quality. The x-axis in both figures is the theoretical amount of training compute, assuming full hardware utilization.

## 6 RELATED WORK

Chen et al. (2020b) and Kong et al. (2020) are two recent works that use diffusion models to produce high fidelity audio conditioned on mel-spectrograms. Concurrent to our work, Chen et al. (2020b) use a combination of improved schedule and _L_ 1 loss to allow sampling with fewer steps with very little reduction in sample quality. However, compared to our unconditional image generation task, their generative task has a strong input conditioning signal provided by the mel-spectrograms, and we hypothesize that this makes it easier to sample with fewer diffusion steps.

Jolicoeur-Martineau et al. (2020) explored score matching in the image domain, and constructed an adversarial training objective to produce better _x_ 0 predictions. However, they found that choosing a better network architecture removed the need for this adversarial objective, suggesting that the adversarial objective is not necessary for powerful generative modeling.

## 7 CONCLUSION

We have shown that, with a few modifications, diffusion models can sample much faster and achieve better log-likelihoods with little impact on sample quality. Here we summarize our main findings:
- Our cosine noise schedule improves NLL (and sometimes FID) compared to the linear schedule from Ho et al. (2020).

- Learning Σ _θ_ using our parameterization and _L_ hybrid objective provides a good trade-off between NLL and FID. More importantly, it allows sampling with many fewer steps without decreased sample quality.
- One can optimize _L_ vlb directly using our importance sampling technique to achieve the best possible NLL at the expense of sample quality.

The combination of these results makes diffusion models an attractive choice for generative modeling, since they combine good log-likelihoods, high-quality samples, and fast sampling with a wellgrounded, stationary training objective. Furthermore, we have investigated how diffusion models scale with the amount of available training compute, and found that more training compute trivially leads to better sample quality and log-likelihood. These results indicate that diffusion models are a promising direction for future research, especially as the affordability of compute increases over time.

The most significant contribution is the hybrid objective function and the reparameterization of the reverse process variance, which elegantly bridges the gap between the simplified "noise prediction" objective and the formal variational lower bound. Overall, this is a high-quality manuscript with strong empirical grounding; the results suggest that diffusion models are not just high-quality sample generators but are also robust density estimators capable of rivaling autoregressive models and VAEs, provided the noise schedules and variance parameterizations are tuned correctly.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper is a highly impactful and well-structured piece of research that effectively addresses a significant gap in the literature: the trade-off between sample quality and log-likelihood in diffusion probabilistic models. The writing is clear, technically rigorous, and appropriately grounded in a strong contextual review of previous work (Ho et al., 2020; Sohl-Dickstein et al., 2015). The authors’ motivation for improving diffusion models—specifically targeting log-likelihood optimization and sampling speed—is compelling, and the proposed innovations (such as the hybrid objective and noise schedule) are described with enough analytical clarity to justify their necessity. However, a potential area of concern is the paper’s heavy reliance on the specific mathematical setup of Ho et al. (2020); while the notation is consistent, the paper would benefit from a more explicit explanation of how their specific "noise schedule" and "reparameterization" interact to prevent the common pitfalls (e.g., instability during the training of the reverse variance). Overall, the work follows a logical trajectory, moving from clear problem identification to systematic technical contributions, which promises to significantly advance the state-of-the-art in generative modeling.

comments.quote: For our experiments, we set \lambda = 0.001 to prevent L_{vlb} from overwhelming L_{simple}.
comments.explanation: The use of an arbitrary constant \lambda without defining the reduction method (e.g., whether loss terms are summed or mean-reduced) renders the hyperparameter choice non-reproducible, as the scale of these objectives likely differs by orders of magnitude.

comments.title: Subjective description of sampling quality trade-offs
comments.quote: However, 100 sampling steps is still sufficient to achieve near-optimal FIDs for our fully trained models.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that 100 steps yield 'near-optimal' FIDs, yet the provided figures show a non-negligible performance gap compared to the 4000-step training baseline. This qualitative descriptor risks misleading readers about the magnitude of the performance trade-off.

progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.label: Progressive Original (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.overall_feedback: This paper is a highly impactful and well-structured piece of research that effectively addresses a significant gap in the literature: the trade-off between sample quality and log-likelihood in diffusion probabilistic models. The writing is clear, technically rigorous, and appropriately grounded in a strong contextual review of previous work (Ho et al., 2020; Sohl-Dickstein et al., 2015). The authors’ motivation for improving diffusion models—specifically targeting log-likelihood optimization and sampling speed—is compelling, and the proposed innovations (such as the hybrid objective and noise schedule) are described with enough analytical clarity to justify their necessity. However, a potential area of concern is the paper’s heavy reliance on the specific mathematical setup of Ho et al. (2020); while the notation is consistent, the paper would benefit from a more explicit explanation of how their specific "noise schedule" and "reparameterization" interact to prevent the common pitfalls (e.g., instability during the training of the reverse variance). Overall, the work follows a logical trajectory, moving from clear problem identification to systematic technical contributions, which promises to significantly advance the state-of-the-art in generative modeling.

comments.title: Broken sentence construction in parameterization description
comments.quote: The most obvious option is to predict _µ_ could then be fed through _θ_ ( _xt, t_ ) directly with a neural network; alternatively, the network could predict _µ_ ˜( _xt, x_ 0); finally, the network could predict the noise _x ϵ_ added to0, and this output _x_ 0, and this noise could be used to predict _x_ 0 via
comments.explanation: The text describing the parameterization of the network appears to have undergone significant OCR corruption. While the intent is clear to a domain expert (predicting the mean, predicting the posterior mean, or predicting the added noise epsilon), the sentence is grammatically nonsensical and misses the concluding parts of the sentences (such as the specific functional mapping for predicting x_0). This level of corruption makes the technical explanation difficult to follow for a graduate reader new to this architecture.

comments.title: Ambiguous/Incomplete definition of the simplified objective
comments.quote: This objective can be seen as a reweighted form of _L_ vlb (without the terms affecting Σ _θ_ ).
comments.explanation: The authors refer to 'Equation 5' or similar in the original paper (implied by context), but in the provided text, the objective is referred to as 'L_simple' and described in the prose without being explicitly defined by an equation. While the context identifies it as the objective from Ho et al. (2020), the absence of this core reweighted objective equation in the text makes it impossible to verify the claim that it is a 'reweighted form of L_vlb'. This is a minor issue as it is common to reference prior work, but given this is the 'Improved' paper, the definition of the baseline objective being improved upon is essential.

comments.title: OCR-corrupted definition of the variance update relation
comments.quote: To go from this definition to variances $\beta_t$, we note that $\beta_t = 1 - \bar{\alpha} \bar{\alpha}_t - 1$
comments.explanation: The text reads '$\beta_t = 1 - \bar{\alpha} \bar{\alpha}_t - 1$'. Based on the standard DDPM definition provided in the summary ($\bar{\alpha}_t = \alpha_t \bar{\alpha}_{t-1}$), the correct relation for calculating $\beta_t$ from $\bar{\alpha}_t$ is $\beta_t = 1 - \frac{\bar{\alpha}_t}{\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}}$. The current formula is clearly an OCR corruption of the fractional form. While a reader familiar with diffusion models can infer the correct equation, this is a technical error in the paper's central methodology section.

comments.title: Implicitly defined skip-step variance re-scaling
comments.quote: Note that $\Sigma_\theta(x_{St}, S_t)$ is parameterized as a range between $\beta_{St}$ and $\tilde{\beta}_{St}$ so it will automatically be rescaled for the shorter diffusion process.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that parameterizing the variance as an interpolation between $\beta_t$ and $\tilde{\beta}_t$ allows it to 'automatically' rescale when using a strided subset of timesteps ($S_t$). However, $\beta_t$ and $\tilde{\beta}_t$ are fixed values associated with the original schedule. If one selects a subset of timesteps, the transition probabilities change, and the corresponding variances for the reverse process should technically be recalculated relative to the new schedule. It is unclear if the network interpolation naturally adapts or if a specific re-mapping logic is required to maintain the validity of the KL divergence calculations under the new schedule.

comments.title: Inconsistent Stride notation
comments.quote: like a strided schedule like ¯ S schedule= (1 , 3 α ¯ ,S 5 t, ..., T , which can be used to obtain corresponding sampling variances − 1)
comments.explanation: The text describing the construction of the strided schedule $S$ is garbled likely due to OCR errors or formatting issues. It interleaves definition elements like '$\bar{\alpha}$' and 'S' in a way that obscures the actual logic of the sequence construction. While a reader can infer that the authors mean a strided subset of the set $\{1, \dots, T\}$, the text as written is mathematically nonsensical and potentially misleading regarding the precise specification of the sampling procedure.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of variance interpolation
comments.quote: Σ θ ( x St, St ) is parameterized as a range between β St and β ˜ St
comments.explanation: The paragraph states that the variance is 'parameterized as a range between $\beta_{St}$ and $\tilde{\beta}_{St}$', but Section 3.1 defined the parameterization as an interpolation in the log domain using an output vector $v$. Stating it is a 'range' is technically imprecise compared to the formal definition (Equation 15 context). While a reader checking Section 3.1 will resolve this, the framing here is imprecise and could lead a reader to believe there is a hard constraint or interval-based optimization, rather than the defined functional output.

comments.title: Ambiguous/Inconsistent Variance Parameterization
comments.quote: Specifically, our model outputs a vector v containing one component per dimension, and we turn this output into variances as follows: ... \Sigma_\theta(x_t, t) = \exp(v \log \beta_t + (1-v) \log \tilde{\beta}_t)
comments.explanation: The formula for variance parameterization in the text leads to a potential inconsistency with the definition of v. In Section 2, the variable v is described as a 'model output vector' used to parameterize the variance. In Equation 14 (referenced in text), this is shown as an interpolation. However, the vector v is not bounded (e.g., via sigmoid). If v is an unconstrained vector, the interpolated variance can easily become negative if a component of v is outside [0, 1]. While the text later mentions, 'We did not apply any constraints on v', it implies the model implicitly learns the interpolation range. If the network outputs extreme values for v, the expression exp(v log beta + (1-v) log beta_tilde) could yield values far outside the meaningful range [beta_tilde, beta], potentially leading to numerical instability during training which contradicts the claim that the current formulation is stable.

comments.title: Potential contradiction in importance sampling justification
comments.quote: Noting that different terms of L_vlb have greatly different magnitudes (Figure 1b), we hypothesized that sampling t uniformly causes unnecessary noise in the L_vlb objective. ... At the beginning of training, we sample t uniformly... With this importance sampled objective, we are able to achieve our best log-likelihoods by optimizing L_vlb.
comments.explanation: The authors perform importance sampling for the summation terms in L_vlb (the KL divergences for each step t) to handle magnitude differences. However, the text says: 'At the beginning of training, we sample t uniformly until we draw 10 samples for every t in [0, T-1]'. The definition of the KL divergence terms for diffusion models usually implies that t ranges from 1 to T or 0 to T-1. An index mismatch exists here: the forward process defines x_1 through x_T, and the reverse process involves T steps. If the index t starts at 0, that implies T+1 steps or a different indexing system than the standard formulation noted in the paper's own introduction (which references x_1...x_T). This ambiguity in index bounds makes the 'importance sampling' implementation slightly underspecified.

comments.title: Overclaim on sampling speed benefit
comments.quote: We find that using more sampling steps becomes increasingly beneficial throughout training. However, 100 sampling steps is still sufficient to achieve near-optimal FIDs for our fully trained models.
comments.explanation: The authors claim 100 steps is sufficient for 'near-optimal' FIDs. Looking at the plots in Figure 5a (ImageNet) and 5c (CIFAR-10), the FID curves do indeed flatten, but there is still a clear vertical gap between 100 steps and 4000 steps (the training baseline). Describing this as 'near-optimal' is a subjective qualitative descriptor that risks misrepresenting the non-negligible gap in absolute FID scores seen in the plots. While the speed-up is real, the implication that quality is largely recovered might mislead a reader regarding the trade-off magnitude.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in variance parameterization description
comments.quote: In particular, our model outputs a vector v containing one component per dimension, and we turn this output into variances as follows: \Sigma_\theta(x_t, t) = \exp(v \log \beta_t + (1-v) \log \tilde{\beta}_t)
comments.explanation: In Section 2, the variable 'v' was defined in the summary as a network output, and the formula given in Section 2.1 (Equation 15 implied) is \Sigma_\theta(x_t, t) = \exp(v \log \beta_t + (1-v) \log \tilde{\beta}_t). While the text describes v as a vector, the math uses it as a scalar weight for the interpolation. Given that the network output is typically a vector of the same dimension as the latent space x to allow for dimension-wise variance prediction, the expression 'v log \beta_t' is algebraically ambiguous: if v is a vector and \beta_t is a scalar, the dimensions don't align for an interpolation of the variance matrix (which is usually diagonal). The lack of explicit broadcasting or index notation for the dimensions makes the implementation of the suggested 'vector v' ambiguous.

comments.title: Ambiguity in loss weight parameterization
comments.quote: For our experiments, we set \lambda = 0.001 to prevent L_{vlb} from overwhelming L_{simple}.
comments.explanation: The hybrid objective is defined as L_{hybrid} = L_{vlb} + \lambda L_{simple}. Depending on the units of the loss (e.g., whether they are averaged over pixels or summed), the magnitude of these terms can differ by orders of magnitude. Providing a specific constant like 0.001 without clarifying the reduction method (mean vs. sum) for L_{vlb} vs L_{simple} makes reproducibility difficult, as the relative impact of the weight depends heavily on this choice.

comments.title: Potential contradiction in importance sampling strategy
comments.quote: At the beginning of training, we sample t uniformly until we draw 10 samples for every t \in [0, T - 1]. With this importance sampled objective, we are able to achieve our best log-likelihoods by optimizing L_{vlb}.
comments.explanation: The authors perform importance sampling for L_{vlb}, but footnote 1 states: 'We found that the importance sampling technique was not helpful when optimizing L_{hybrid} directly.' This creates a potential conceptual gap. If L_{hybrid} includes L_{vlb}, and L_{vlb} suffers from high variance (the motivation for importance sampling), why would the combined objective 'Hybrid'—which incorporates L_{vlb}—not benefit from the same variance reduction? The lack of explanation suggests the interaction between the two objectives within the 'Hybrid' formulation (specifically the stop-gradient on the mean) may be doing more to stabilize training than the authors explicitly attribute to it.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This manuscript addresses a fundamental limitation of denoising diffusion probabilistic models by demonstrating that competitive log-likelihoods can be achieved alongside high sample quality, a gap identified in prior work. The authors propose a set of modifications, including a hybrid learning objective, a novel noise schedule, and a variance reparameterization, which collectively enable faster sampling with an order-of-magnitude reduction in steps while improving log-likelihood performance on high-diversity datasets. The writing is generally clear and the motivation is well-articulated

comments.title: Conceptually incorrect reference to distribution extremes in Sec 2.2
comments.quote: They found that they achieve similar sample quality using either _σt_[2][=] _[β][t]_[or] _[ σ] t_[2][=] _[β]_[˜] _[t]_[, which are] two extremes given by _q_ ( _x_ 0) being either isotropic Gaussian noise or a delta function, respectively.
comments.explanation: Initially, the phrase 'given by q(x_0) being either isotropic Gaussian noise or a delta function' caught my eye because $q(x_0)$ denotes the fixed data distribution, which is neither isotropic Gaussian nor a delta function. I checked the surrounding context and the standard DDPM derivation to see if this was a typo for $q(x_T|x_0)$ or the posterior $q(x_{t-1}|x_t, x_0)$. Even assuming the most plausible intended symbol (e.g., $q(x_T|x_0)$ or the reverse process variance), the statement remains conceptually confused: $ eta_t$ and $	ilde{ eta}_t$ are schedule-derived variance scalars for the forward and posterior steps, respectively. They are not extremes of a conditional data distribution. The authors likely meant these are two extremes for the variance of the approximate reverse process $p_	heta(x_{t-1}|x_t)$ at the final step, or they were misquoting a relation about the prior/posterior. This misstatement could mislead readers about why fixing the variance to these constants is justified for sample quality.

comments.title: Underspecified importance sampling update rule in Sec 3.3
comments.quote: Since _E_ [ _L_[2] _t_[]][is][unknown][beforehand][and][may][change][throughout][training,][we][maintain][a][history] of the previous 10 values for each loss term, and update this dynamically during training. At the beginning of training, we sample _t_ uniformly until we draw 10 samples for every _t ∈_ [0 _,_ T −_ 1].
comments.explanation: The authors describe using importance sampling to reduce gradient noise by weighting timestep sampling probabilities based on $\mathbb{E}[L_t^2]$. However, the update mechanism for these probabilities is underspecified. 'Maintaining a history of the previous 10 values' is ambiguous: is this a strict FIFO buffer of size 10, an exponential moving average with a window of 10 steps, or something else? Furthermore, the text does not explain how these 10 stored values are aggregated (e.g., mean, median, max) to compute the importance weights, nor how the weights are normalized or updated after each training step. Without this detail, a practitioner cannot faithfully reproduce the reported log-likelihood improvements. The uniform warmup phase is clear, but the ongoing dynamic update is missing.

comments.title: Ambiguous indexing of the sparse sampling schedule in Sec 4
comments.quote: We can compute _p_ ( _xSt−_ 1 _|xSt_ ) as _N_ ( _µθ_ ( _xSt, St_ ) _,_ Σ _θ_ ( _xSt, St_ )).
comments.explanation: The paper defines a sequence $S$ of timesteps for faster sampling, but then switches notation to $S_t$ and $S_{t-1}$ in the reverse distribution formula. It is unclear whether $S_t$ refers to the $t$-th element of the schedule array $S$ (e.g., $S_0=1, S_1=3, \dots$), or if it denotes a continuously interpolated timestep value scaled to the original $T$-step process. In optimization literature, $S_t$ often implies a continuous variable or a fixed mapping, while here it seems to index an array. This notational clash could lead readers to incorrectly implement the sampling step or miscalculate the corresponding noise schedule values ($\bar{\alpha}_{S_t}$) when computing $\mu_\theta$ and $\Sigma_\theta$. Clarifying whether $S$ is 0-indexed, how many steps are skipped, and how the network's time embedding $t$ is normalized would resolve this.

comments.title: Severe OCR degradation obscures key mathematical descriptions in Sec 2.2 and Sec 4
comments.quote: The most obvious option is to predict _µ_ could then be fed through _θ_ ( _xt, t_ ) directly with a neural network; alternatively, the network could predict _µ_ ˜( _xt, x_ 0); finally, the network could predict the noise _x ϵ_ added to0, and this output _x_ 0, and this noise could be used to predict _x_ 0 via
comments.explanation: While I recognize this passage is heavily corrupted by PDF-to-OCR extraction, the resulting text is so degraded that it obscures the three standard parameterization options for diffusion models (predicting $\mu_\theta$, $\tilde{\mu}_\theta$, or $\epsilon$). The mangled phrasing ('predict _µ_ could then be fed through _θ_... predict the noise _x ϵ_ added to0') makes it difficult for a careful reader to parse the authors' intent without external knowledge of the DDPM paper. Although forward references and overview sections are granted leniency, this specific paragraph introduces the core modeling choices that justify the rest of the improvements. If a reader relies solely on this text, they may misinterpret how the network output is mapped to the latent variable, which could cascade into confusion when reading the variance parameterization and loss weighting discussions that follow.

comments.title: Underspecified importance sampling procedure
comments.quote: Since E[L_t^2] is unknown beforehand and may change throughout training, we maintain a history of the previous 10 values for each loss term, and update this dynamically during training.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly identify that uniform sampling introduces unnecessary gradient noise due to the varying magnitudes of VLB terms across timesteps, and they propose importance sampling to address this. However, the description of how the sampling distribution p(t) is constructed is incomplete. The text mentions maintaining a 'history of the previous 10 values' but omits critical reproducibility details: how these values are aggregated (e.g., simple average vs. exponential moving average), what the exact weighting function is (standard practice uses p(t) ∝ √E[L_t^2] rather than raw E[L_t^2]), and how the distribution is normalized over t ∈ [0, T]. Without these specifications, a careful reader cannot accurately reproduce the sampling schedule or understand why a history length of exactly 10 was chosen. This is a moderate gap that directly impacts the reproducibility of the log-likelihood results.

comments.title: Contradictory phrasing regarding unconstrained variance interpolation
comments.quote: We did not apply any constraints on v, theoretically allowing the model to predict variances outside of the interpolated range. However, we did not observe the network doing this in practice, suggesting that the bounds for Σ_θ(x_t, t) are indeed expressive enough.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly and cleverly propose parameterizing the reverse variance as an interpolation between β_t and β̃_t in the log domain, controlled by a vector v. They note that v is left unconstrained, which theoretically allows the network to predict variances outside the [β_t, β̃_t] range. However, their conclusion that this empirical behavior suggests the 'bounds' are 'expressive enough' is logically inconsistent. If v is unconstrained, there are no mathematical bounds to be expressive or restrictive; the parameterization is theoretically unbounded. What they likely mean is that the interpolation range itself is sufficiently expressive to cover the optimal variance, so the network naturally stays within it. This slight non-sequitur could mislead a reader into thinking the parameterization implicitly enforces bounds, when in fact it does not. It is a minor framing issue that does not undermine the method but warrants clearer phrasing.

comments.title: Confusion between stride index and step count in timestep range description
comments.quote: To address this, we use a strided subset of timesteps as for FID (with stride _K_ ), but we also include every _t_ from 1 to _T/K_ . This requires _T/K_ extra evaluation steps, but greatly improves log-likelihood compared to the uniformly strided schedule.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly identify that uniformly striding the diffusion schedule harms log-likelihood because it discards critical early timesteps, which contribute disproportionately to the variational lower bound (as shown in Fig. 1b and standard diffusion literature). Their intent to recover these early steps is sound and aligns with the claim that the hybrid objective helps maintain quality at low step counts. However, the mathematical description of the timestep range contains a notational slip. A stride $K$ means the step size is $K$, so the strided schedule selects $t \in \{T, T-K, T-2K, \dots\}$, making the earliest sampled timestep approximately $K$ (or $T \bmod K$). The text states they add timesteps "from 1 to $T/K$". Since $T/K$ is the *number* of strided steps, not the *timestep index*, this bound incorrectly extends past the earliest strided step. For example, with $T=4000$ and $K=40$, the strided schedule starts at $t=40$, but the text suggests adding $t \in [1, 100]$ ($T/K$), which redundantly covers $t \in [41, 100]$ and mischaracterizes the missing range. The intended bound is almost certainly $t \in [1, K]$ (or $t \in [1, \text{stride}]$). This inconsistency is internal to the paragraph (they note it requires $T/K$ extra steps, matching the bound they wrote), but it mixes step count with timestep indexing, which could mislead reproducers into adding too many redundant steps or misunderstanding which timesteps drive the likelihood improvement. The fix is straightforward: replace $T/K$ with $K$ (or explicitly state the stride value) in the timestep bound.

comments.title: Typographical error in author name for Glow citation
comments.quote: Durk P Kingma and Prafullar Dhariwal. Glow: Generative flow with invertible 1x1 convolutions. In _Advances in neural information processing systems_ , pp. 10215–10224, 2018.
comments.explanation: Upon reviewing the bibliography, the first author of the Glow paper is listed as 'Prafullar Dhariwal'. The correct spelling is 'Prafulla Dhariwal'. This appears to be an OCR or typographical artifact. While it does not impact the methodology, equations, or ability to reproduce results, bibliographic accuracy is important for proper attribution and citation linking. This should be corrected to match the published literature.

comments.title: Potential title mismatch for Jolicoeur-Martineau et al. (2020)
comments.quote: Alexia Jolicoeur-Martineau, Rémi Piché-Taillefer, Rémi Tachet des Combes, and Ioannis Mitliagkas. Adversarial score matching and improved sampling for image generation, 2020.
comments.explanation: The reference title listed here differs from the widely known published version of this work, which is 'Relaxed adversarial score matching' (ICLR 2021). It is possible this reflects an earlier preprint title, but as written, it may cause confusion or citation mismatches for readers searching the bibliography. I recommend verifying the final published title against the arXiv/ICLR record and updating accordingly to ensure accurate academic linkage.

Their intent to recover these early steps is sound and aligns with the claim that the hybrid objective helps maintain quality at low step counts. However, the mathematical description of the timestep range contains a notational slip. A stride $K$ means the step size is $K$, so the strided schedule selects $t \in \{T, T-K, T-2K, \dots\}$, making the earliest sampled timestep approximately $K$ (or $T \bmod K$). The text states they add timesteps "from 1 to $T/K$". Since $T/K$ is the *number* of strided steps, not the *timestep index*, this bound incorrectly extends past the earliest strided step. For example, with $T=4000$ and $K=40$, the strided schedule starts at $t=40$, but the text suggests adding $t \in [1, 100]$ ($T/K$), which redundantly covers $t \in [41, 100]$ and mischaracterizes the missing range. The intended bound is almost certainly $t \in [1, K]$ (or $t \in [1, \text{stride}]$). This inconsistency is internal to the paragraph (they note it requires $T/K$ extra steps, matching the bound they wrote), but it mixes step count with timestep indexing, which could mislead reproducers into adding too many redundant steps or misunderstanding which timesteps drive the likelihood improvement. The fix is straightforward: replace $T/K$ with $K$ (or explicitly state the stride value) in the timestep bound.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)

The writing is clear, with standard academic prose and consistent mathematical notation for defining the forward/reverse processes and the Variational Lower Bound (VLB). However, the most significant issue is that the provided text is incomplete; the draft abruptly cuts off mid-sentence in the beginning of Section 3. As a result, it is impossible to evaluate the specific technical contributions (such as the proposed hybrid objective and noise schedule) or assess the claimed results regarding scaling and sampling efficiency. A full review cannot be conducted without the complete methodology and experimental sections.

comments.title: Mathematical Inconsistency and Mischaracterization in Cosine Schedule
comments.quote: To address this, we construct a different noise schedule in terms of \bar{\alpha}_t: \alpha_t = \frac{\sin((t+s)/(T+s)\\pi/2)^2}{\sin((s+1)/(T+s)\\pi/2)^2}
comments.explanation: The provided equation for the cosine schedule implies that the coefficient \alpha_t increases as t increases (since the numerator \sin^2(...) goes from 0 to 1, while the denominator is constant), or if interpreted as \bar{\alpha}_t, implies it starts at 0 and ends at 1 (reconstruction schedule). Neither matches the standard diffusion process where noise should increase, which requires \alpha_t (the fraction of original signal preserved) to decrease (i.e., go from ~1 to ~0). Additionally, the standard formulation ensures a 'linear drop-off' (in practice of \bar{\alpha}_t or \alpha_t) in the middle. The equation in the text appears to be an OCR error or mathematical misstatement that contradicts the descriptive claim of a decay schedule.

comments.title: Contradictory Sampling Step Count Description
comments.quote: we use a stride K over timesteps to reduce the total number of sampling steps from T to T/K . This requires T/K extra evaluation steps
comments.explanation: The text claims to first reduce the total number of sampling steps to T/K and then add T/K extra steps, which mathematically implies a total of approximately 2*(T/K). However, the text immediately following states that 100 sampling steps is sufficient, creating a contradiction (e.g., if T=4000 and K=40, T/K=100, but the 'extra steps' description implies ~200). Furthermore, the description of the 'strided subset' used for this process is ambiguous regarding whether the set {1, ..., T/K} overlaps with the strided steps, which likely contributes to this calculation error and confusion for a reader attempting to reproduce the schedule.

comments.explanation: The reference list contains two errors regarding the paper 'PixelSNAIL'. First, it cites the venue as 'International Conference on Machine Learning' in '2018', whereas the paper was actually published at 'International Conference on Learning Representations' (ICLR) in 2019. Second, the author list omits 'Yiding Jiang', who should be the final author.

comments.title: Misattribution of Diffusion Efficiency Claim
comments.quote: Chen et al. (2020b) found that diffusion models can efficiently generate audio using a small number of sampling steps
comments.explanation: The paper cites 'Chen et al. (2020b)' [Wavegrad] to support the claim that diffusion models can efficiently generate audio. However, the finding of efficient sampling in diffusion models for audio is primarily associated with 'Diffwave' (by Zhifeng Kong et al.), while Wavegrad is a score-based generative model. The authors have misattributed this finding to the wrong paper.

comments.title: Variable Definition in Schedule Formula
comments.quote: \beta_t = 1 _− α_ ¯ _αt_ ¯ _−t_ 1[.][In practice, we clip] _[ β][t]_[to be] no larger than 0.999 to prevent singularities
comments.explanation: The OCR extraction has garbled the fraction bar in the definition of \beta_t. The intended formula is clearly \beta_t = 1 - \frac{\bar{\alpha}_t}{\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}}, which is the correct definition derived from the cumulative product relation. This is a technical error in the transcription, but the mathematical content is correct and immediately inferable from standard notation.

comments.title: Inconsistency in the description of the sampling step count
comments.quote: we use a stride K over timesteps to reduce the total number of sampling steps from T to T/K . This requires T/K extra evaluation steps
comments.explanation: The text claims to first reduce the total number of sampling steps to T/K and then add T/K extra steps, which mathematically implies the total number of steps is approximately 2*(T/K). However, the text immediately following states, 'However, 100 sampling steps is still sufficient to achieve near-optimal FIDs.' Given that the models are trained with T=4000 steps, T/K would be 100 if K=40. This creates a contradiction: if the method requires 'T/K extra' steps, one would expect the total steps to be roughly 200, not 100. This makes it difficult to determine the exact sampling budget described by the text and contradicts the implication of the result.

comments.title: Ambiguous description of the 'strided subset' implementation
comments.quote: we use a strided subset of timesteps as for FID (with stride K), but we also include every t from 1 to T/K
comments.explanation: The description of the schedule is mathematically ambiguous. It is unclear if the 'strided subset' (used for FID) and the set {1, ..., T/K} overlap or are disjoint. If the stride K implies arithmetic steps (e.g., 1, 1+K, 1+2K...), the sets {1, ..., T/K} and the strided set would overlap significantly (e.g., if K=40 and T=4000, strided set contains 1, 41, 81..., and {1, ..., 100} contains 1, ..., 100). The statement 'This requires T/K extra evaluation steps' suggests the author assumes non-overlapping additions, but the text does not specify the exact composition of the 'strided subset' defined in the preceding (garbled) section, potentially causing confusion for a reader attempting to reproduce the schedule.

comments.title: Misattribution of diffusion model efficiency claim
comments.quote: Chen et al. (2020b) found that diffusion models can efficiently generate audio using a small number of sampling steps
comments.explanation: The Introduction (and the summary provided in the context) cites 'Chen et al. (2020b)' to support the claim that 'diffusion models can efficiently generate audio'. However, the reference list for (2020b) is 'Nanxin Chen, Yu Zhang... Wavegrad'. 'Wavegrad' is a score-based generative model (using Langevin dynamics/gradient estimation), not a denoising diffusion probabilistic model (which uses score matching with isotropic Gaussian noise). The diffusion model that demonstrates efficiency for audio is 'Zhifeng Kong... Diffwave'. The authors appear to have confused or misattributed the finding to the wrong paper.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper is well-structured and clearly motivated, successfully identifying key limitations in prior diffusion models—namely, suboptimal log-likelihoods, poor scaling to high-diversity datasets, and slow sampling—and proposing a coherent set of modifications to address them. The writing is generally clear, though the exposition in Section 2 is rather dense and closely follows Ho et al. without providing much novel intuition or pedagogical value; a more concise or reorganized treatment would improve readability. The most significant issue at this stage is the lack of detail on the central methodological contributions—the hybrid objective, the reparameterization of the reverse process variance, and the novel noise schedule—which are only mentioned in passing. The surprising claim that optimizing the hybrid objective yields better log-likelihoods than direct likelihood optimization is stated without any supporting evidence or explanation in the excerpt, leaving the reader skeptical and demanding strong empirical validation. Additionally, the paper’s assertion of “competitive log-likelihoods” is vague without explicit baselines or datasets; it should be clarified what is meant by “competitive” (e.g., relative to VAEs, autoregressive models, or GANs) and which specific metrics and datasets are intended. Overall, the introduction effectively frames the contributions, but the paper must flesh out the technical details and provide rigorous comparisons to substantiate its bold claims.

comments.title: Undefined β0 and possible off-by-one indexing error in cosine schedule
comments.quote: The small offset _s_ in our schedule prevents _βt_ from being too small near _t_ = 0, since we found that having tiny amounts of noise at the beginning of the process made it hard for the network to predict _ϵ_ accurately enough. In particular, we selected _s_ such that _[√] β_ 0 was slightly smaller than the pixel bin size, 1 _/_ 127 _._ 5.
comments.explanation: The forward process is formally defined for steps t = 1,...,T, with variance β_t. The cosine schedule defines ᾱ_t for t = 0,...,T, and β_t = 1 - ᾱ_t/ᾱ_{t-1} is defined only for t ≥ 1 (since ᾱ_{-1} is undefined). The authors refer to β_0, which is not defined in the notation summary and would correspond to an extrapolated value. The intended meaning is to adjust the first actual diffusion step (likely β_1), but the notation is formally incorrect and could confuse readers implementing the schedule. This is a minor notational inconsistency.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrase 'order of magnitude fewer diffusion steps'
comments.quote: Additionally, our models allow for sampling with an order of magnitude fewer diffusion steps with only a modest difference in sample quality.
comments.explanation: The phrase "order of magnitude fewer diffusion steps" is ambiguous: does it mean exactly 10x fewer steps (e.g., from 1000 to 100), or roughly a factor of 10? The paper later (in Section 4) discusses reducing sampling steps but does not specify a precise factor in the abstract. In the results, Table 1 and the surrounding text use _T_ = 4000 for training, but Section 4's exploration of reducing steps claims "a number of seconds rather than minutes" without a specific ratio. The ambiguity could lead a reader to overestimate the speedup. Since the paper provides concrete numbers elsewhere (e.g., 100 steps in the discussion around Figure 3a), the claim is supported but not quantified in the abstract. This is a minor framing concern.

comments.title: Potential overclaim: 'competitive log-likelihoods... even on high-diversity datasets like ImageNet'
comments.quote: In this paper, we show that diffusion models can achieve competitive log-likelihoods while maintaining good sample quality, even on high-diversity datasets like ImageNet.
comments.explanation: The claim asserts that the models achieve 'competitive log-likelihoods' on ImageNet. However, Table 3 later shows that while their model (3.53 bits/dim) improves over the baseline (3.77) and approaches some prior models, it still lags behind the best transformer-based models (e.g., Sparse Transformer at 3.44, RoutingTransformer at 3.43). 'Competitive' is somewhat subjective—these results are close but not state-of-the-art. Given the abstract's more moderate language ('can achieve competitive log-likelihoods'), this is a mild overclaim in the introduction but is contextualized later in Table 3. I consider this a minor issue.

comments.title: Unclear derivation of sampling noise schedule for reduced steps
comments.quote:
Given the training noise schedule _αt_ , we can obtain the sampling noise

We can compute _p_ ( _xSt−_ 1 _|xSt_ ) as _N_ ( _µθ_ ( _xSt, St_ ) _,_ Σ _θ_ ( _xSt, St_ )). Note that Σ _θ_ ( _xSt, St_ ) is parameterized as a range between _βSt_ and _β_[˜] _St_ so it will automatically be rescaled for the shorter diffusion process.
comments.explanation: The passage in Section 4 describes using a 'strided schedule' to sample with fewer steps, but the derivation of the corresponding sampling variances is incomplete. The text states 'Given the training noise schedule _αt_ , we can obtain the sampling noise' but then cuts off before showing how. The next sentence jumps to computing _p_ ( _xSt−1|xSt_ ) using the same _µθ_ and Σ _θ_ at step _St_ , but it's not explicit how the variance should be adjusted for the skipped steps (e.g., whether to use the cumulative variance over the gap). This is a non-trivial detail: in Ho et al. (2020), the variance for a sub-sequence requires careful handling of the process's Markov property. The paper later mentions that Σ _θ_ is 'automatically rescaled' because it's parameterized as a range, but this is vague—how exactly does the interpolation between _β_ and _β̃_ scale? Without further specification, a reader attempting to reproduce the method would not have enough information. This is an underspecified method.

comments.title: Importance sampling range excludes the final VLB term
comments.quote: we maintain a history of the previous 10 values for each loss term, and update this dynamically during training. At the beginning of training, we sample t uniformly until we draw 10 samples for every t ∈ [0, T-1].
comments.explanation: The variational lower bound consists of terms L_0, L_1, ..., L_{T-1}, L_T. The text specifies importance sampling over t ∈ [0, T-1], which would exclude L_T. While L_T is typically negligible (it does not depend on θ and is close to zero), the description does not justify its omission. Additionally, L_0 is a discretized likelihood term and might have a different functional form than the other KL divergences; the uniform initialization strategy treats it identically to the other terms. The oversight is minor because in practice L_T is very small and the uniform sampling initialization will eventually be overridden by the stored history, but a careful reader might wonder whether L_T is intentionally excluded and whether this choice affects the gradient noise reduction claims.

comments.title: Pixel bin size calculation appears inconsistent with common data scaling
comments.quote: we selected _s_ such that _[√] β_ 0 was slightly smaller than the pixel bin size, 1 _/_ 127 _._ 5.
comments.explanation: For image data with 256 intensity levels, a typical preprocessing is to scale pixel values to [-1,1], which gives a bin size of 2/256 = 1/128 ≈ 0.0078125. The paper states the bin size as 1/127.5 ≈ 0.007843, which is slightly larger. Unless the paper uses a different scaling (e.g., [0,1] or a different discretization), this value is inconsistent with the standard convention. The offset s is chosen to make √β_0 slightly smaller than this bin size, so the discrepancy could affect the resulting schedule. The difference is small but suggests a possible arithmetic error or undefined preprocessing detail.

comments.title: Ambiguity in strided schedule for log-likelihood evaluation
comments.quote: we use a strided subset of timesteps as for FID (with stride _K_ ), but we also include every _t_ from 1 to _T/K_ . This requires _T/K_ extra evaluation steps, but greatly improves log-likelihood compared to the uniformly strided schedule.
comments.explanation: The description is ambiguous. It says they use a strided subset of timesteps (as for FID) and also include every t from 1 to T/K. This leaves unclear whether the strided subset already includes some early steps, leading to possible overlap. The phrase 'as for FID' does not specify the starting point of the stride. Without additional details, a reader cannot precisely reproduce the evaluation schedule. While the intent is plausible, the method is underspecified.

comments.title: Potential handling of non-integer strides in sampling step reduction
comments.quote: we use a stride _K_ over timesteps to reduce the total number of sampling steps from _T_ to _T/K_ . In Figures 5a and 5c, we evaluate FIDs for an _L_ hybrid model and an _L_ simple model that were trained with 4000 diffusion steps, using 30, 50, 100, 150, 200, 400, and 4000 sampling steps.
comments.explanation: The text implies that the number of sampling steps is exactly T/K, which requires K to be a divisor of T. However, 4000/30 is not an integer, and similarly 4000/150 is not integer (26.67). The method for selecting exactly 30 or 150 steps from 4000 total steps using a constant stride is not explained. It could be that a slightly different stride or rounding is used, or that the steps are chosen as equally spaced indices rather than a fixed integer stride. This lack of clarity could hinder reproduction of the FID evaluations.

comments.title: Inconsistent terminology: 'depth' instead of 'width' for channel multiplier
comments.quote: Since the depth of each layer affects the scale of the initial weights, we scale the Adam learning rate for each model by 1 _/[√]_ channel multiplier
comments.explanation: The phrase 'depth of each layer' appears to be a misnomer. The preceding sentence explains that they vary model capacity by applying a multiplier such that the first layer has 64, 96, 128, or 192 channels, which is a change in layer width (number of channels), not depth (number of layers). The subsequent use of 'channel multiplier' clarifies the intent, but the initial wording could confuse a reader about whether they are modifying the number of layers or the channels per layer.

comments.quote: They found that they achieve similar sample quality using either _σt_[2][=] _[β][t]_[or] _[ σ] t_[2][=] _[β]_[˜] _[t]_[, which are] two extremes given by _q_ ( _x_ 0) being either isotropic Gaussian noise or a delta function, respectively.
comments.explanation: This is a minor OCR artifact: 'or _σt_2 = _β̃t_' is rendered as '[or] [_σ_] t[2][=] _[β]_[˜] _[t]_'. The brackets and spacing are garbled, but the meaning is clear from context. Not a substantive issue.

comments.title: Possible off-by-one indexing error for β₀ in cosine schedule description
comments.quote: In particular, we selected s such that √β_0 was slightly smaller than the pixel bin size, 1/127.5.
comments.explanation: The cosine schedule is defined in terms of ᾱ_t for t = 0,...,T, and β_t = 1 - ᾱ_t/ᾱ_{t-1} is defined only for t ≥ 1 (since ᾱ_{-1} is undefined). The authors likely intend √β_1 or √β_t for the first diffusion step (t=1), not β_0. The text says 'near t=0' but then writes √β_0. If β_0 were interpreted literally (e.g., variance at the initial data point), it would be zero and the condition would be trivially false. The intended meaning is clear from context—adjusting s so that the first variance step is appropriately small—but the notation is formally incorrect and could confuse a reader implementing the schedule.

comments.title: Importance sampling range excludes the final VLB term
comments.quote: we maintain a history of the previous 10 values for each loss term, and update this dynamically during training. At the beginning of training, we sample t uniformly until we draw 10 samples for every t ∈ [0, T-1].
comments.explanation: The variational lower bound consists of terms L_0, L_1, ..., L_{T-1}, L_T. The text specifies importance sampling over t ∈ [0, T-1], which would exclude L_T. While L_T is typically negligible (it does not depend on θ and is close to zero), the description does not justify its omission. Additionally, L_0 is a discretized likelihood term and might have a different functional form than the other KL divergences; the uniform initialization strategy treats it identically to the other terms. The oversight is minor because in practice L_T is very small and the uniform sampling initialization will eventually be overridden by the stored history, but a careful reader might wonder whether L_T is intentionally excluded and whether this choice affects the gradient noise reduction claims.

comments.title: Notational inconsistency: β_0 outside defined range
comments.quote: The small offset _s_ in our schedule prevents _βt_ from being too small near _t_ = 0, since we found that having tiny amounts of noise at the beginning of the process made it hard for the network to predict _ϵ_ accurately enough. In particular, we selected _s_ such that _[√] β_ 0 was slightly smaller than the pixel bin size, 1 _/_ 127 _._ 5.
comments.explanation: The forward process is formally defined for steps t = 1,...,T, with variance β_t. Here the authors refer to β_0, which is not defined in the notation summary. While one can imagine an extended continuous index t ∈ [0,T], the discrete schedule does not specify β_0, and it is ambiguous whether β_0 corresponds to β_1 or an extrapolated value. This can confuse readers trying to replicate the cosine schedule computation, as the clipping and scaling decisions depend on this value.

comments.title: Pixel bin size calculation appears inconsistent with common data scaling
comments.quote: we selected _s_ such that _[√] β_ 0 was slightly smaller than the pixel bin size, 1 _/_ 127 _._ 5.
comments.explanation: For image data with 256 intensity levels, a typical preprocessing is to scale pixel values to [-1,1], which gives a bin size of 2/256 = 1/128 ≈ 0.0078125. The paper states the bin size as 1/127.5 ≈ 0.007843, which is slightly larger. Unless the paper uses a different scaling (e.g., [0,1] or a different discretization), this value is inconsistent with the standard convention. The offset s is chosen to make √β_0 slightly smaller than this bin size, so the discrepancy could affect the resulting schedule. The difference is small but suggests a possible arithmetic error or undefined preprocessing detail.

comments.title: Ambiguity in strided schedule for log-likelihood evaluation
comments.quote: we use a strided subset of timesteps as for FID (with stride _K_ ), but we also include every _t_ from 1 to _T/K_ . This requires _T/K_ extra evaluation steps, but greatly improves log-likelihood compared to the uniformly strided schedule.
comments.explanation: The description is ambiguous. It says they use a strided subset of timesteps (as for FID) and also include every t from 1 to T/K. This leaves unclear whether the strided subset already includes some early steps, leading to possible overlap. The phrase 'as for FID' does not specify the starting point of the stride. Without additional details, a reader cannot precisely reproduce the evaluation schedule. While the intent is plausible, the method is underspecified.

comments.title: Potential handling of non-integer strides in sampling step reduction
comments.quote: we use a stride _K_ over timesteps to reduce the total number of sampling steps from _T_ to _T/K_ . In Figures 5a and 5c, we evaluate FIDs for an _L_ hybrid model and an _L_ simple model that were trained with 4000 diffusion steps, using 30, 50, 100, 150, 200, 400, and 4000 sampling steps.
comments.explanation: The text implies that the number of sampling steps is exactly T/K, which requires K to be a divisor of T. However, 4000/30 is not an integer, and similarly 4000/150 is not integer (26.67). The method for selecting exactly 30 or 150 steps from 4000 total steps using a constant stride is not explained. It could be that a slightly different stride or rounding is used, or that the steps are chosen as equally spaced indices rather than a fixed integer stride. This lack of clarity could hinder reproduction of the FID evaluations.

comments.title: Inconsistent terminology: 'depth' instead of 'width' for channel multiplier
comments.quote: Since the depth of each layer affects the scale of the initial weights, we scale the Adam learning rate for each model by 1 _/[√]_ channel multiplier
comments.explanation: The phrase 'depth of each layer' appears to be a misnomer. The preceding sentence explains that they vary model capacity by applying a multiplier such that the first layer has 64, 96, 128, or 192 channels, which is a change in layer width (number of channels), not depth (number of layers). The subsequent use of 'channel multiplier' clarifies the intent, but the initial wording could confuse a reader about whether they are modifying the number of layers or the channels per layer.
